comments.text: personally I find #181 more relevant (compared to #576). naive DRC says that $\approx 2^n(1-c)^{-n}$ vertices should at least suffice. and for fixed $c>0$, the arguments of Tikhomirov can show there is $c'\in (0,c)$ so that $2^n (1-c')^{-n}$ vertices suffice for large $n$.

I'll note that the "blow-up case" should be clear here (at least if you want $m=(1+o(1))2^n$ vertices). let $H_0$ be any $m_0$ graph with minimum degree $>m_0/2$. by Dirac's theorem, $H_0$ should contain a Hamilton cycle, $C=v_1,\dots,v_{m_0}$. now, consider consider the homomorphism $\phi$ which sends the $i$-th layer of the hypercube to $v_{i\pmod{m_0}}$. this is clearly a homomorphism, and the pre-image of any vertex has size $\sim 2^n/m_0$ (since the binomial coefficeints do not vary significantly).
actually, maybe by an appropriately quantified blow-up lemma, we can actually get a $(1+o(1))2^n$ bound in general?
What do you mean by a blow-up lemma in this case?

"the blow-up lemma" is a very deep result about doing spanning or nearly spanning embeddings of bounded degree $H$ into graphs $G$, based off of the regularity partition of $G$ (essentially if various parameters are right, we can embed $H$ as long as it has homomorphism into a certain auxiliary graph based on the partition). usually the bounds of various dependencies are fairly poor. but since we are embedding something bipartite (and just using pathwidth, rather than a more complicated), I could fathom it being possible for the bounds to work out (at least if we are willing to just aim for a nearly spanning embedding).
Aha, you meant THAT blow-up lemma, just with actually good dependencies. Thanks!
